channel.title: Yury's Reading List
channel.description: Articles, posts, and blogs curated by Yury

item.title: What Held Up at 3 AM: One Engineer's RAG Case Study
item.description: Ship RAG with Weave CLI: Opik traces, an LLM-judge eval harness, and config-driven swaps across 11 vector databases, chunking, and agents.

item.title: The Pulse: AI load breaks GitHub – why not other vendors?
item.description: Also: Anthropic’s speed run to break devs’ goodwill, big price increases from GitHub Copilot, Mitchell Hashimoto on the “building block economy,” and more

item.title: Company Brain: Why Most Companies Have Data But No Memory
item.description: One of the hardest parts of any organization is institutional friction. Conversations lose context. Meetings create ambiguous follow-ups. Pe...

item.title: Guardians of the Agents
item.description: Agentic applications—AI systems empowered to take autonomous actions by calling external tools—are the current rage in software development. They promise efficiency, convenience, and reduced human intervention. Giving autonomous agents access to tools with potentially irreversible side effects, however, comes with significant risks. These dangers can originate from adversarial models trying to optimize their objectives literally (e.g., maximizing the number of paper clips at the expense of human

item.title: A forty-year career.
item.description: The Silicon Valley narrative centers on entrepreneurial protagonists who are poised one predestined step away from changing the world. A decade ago they were heroes, and more recently they’ve become villains, but either way they are absolutely the protagonists. Working within the industry, I’ve worked with quite a few non-protagonists who experience their time in technology differently: a period of obligatory toil required to pry open the gate to the American Dream.

item.title: Sizing engineering teams.
item.description: I’ve come to believe that most organizational design questions can be answered by recursively applying a framework for sizing teams. Over the past year I’ve refined my approach to team sizing into a bit of a framework, and even changed my mind on several aspects, especially the viability of small teams. This post describes how I now size teams

item.title: Building Pi, and what makes self-modifying software so fascinating
item.description: Mario Zechner, creator of Pi, joins Armin Ronacher to explore AI coding’s limits, arguing that human judgment still matters most in an agent-driven world.

item.title: How will AI change operating systems? Part 1: Ubuntu and Linux
item.description: A deepdive with the Canonical team into how AI is changing Ubuntu, why they’re betting on local-first LLMs, and a look into other Linux distributions

item.title: The Pulse: AI token spending out of control – what’s next?
item.description: Details from 15 tech companies on the rapid growth of token spend, and their responses to it. Also: AI vendors can’t keep up with demand, plummeting morale at Meta, and more.

item.title: Designing Data-intensive Applications with Martin Kleppmann
item.description: Martin Kleppmann on scaling, his updated Designing Data-Intensive Applications, and what’s next for AI-era systems.

item.title: How LiteParse's Grid Projection Algorithm Parses PDFs
item.description: A deep dive into LiteParse's grid projection algorithm — how it extracts text from PDFs while preserving tables, columns, and alignment. Open source.

item.title: There Are Only 6 RAG Evals
item.description: There are only 6 fundamental ways to evaluate a RAG system. Here's how to do it.

item.title: Evaluating the Effectiveness of LLM-Evaluators (aka LLM-as-Judge)
item.description: Use cases, techniques, alignment, finetuning, and critiques against LLM-evaluators.

item.title: Demystifying evals for AI agents
item.description: Good evaluations help teams ship AI agents more confidently. Without them, it’s easy to get stuck in reactive loops—catching issues only in production, where fixing one failure creates others. Evals make problems and behavioral changes visible before they affect users, and their value compounds over the lifecycle of an agent.

item.title: Learnings from conducting ~1,000 interviews at Amazon
item.description: Steve Huynh, formerly Principal Engineer at Amazon, shares observations from 10+ years of interviewing software engineers, and an excerpt from his new book, Technical Behavioral Interview

item.title: Metadata: the 3rd kind of retrieval
item.description: In search we talk about lexical embedding retrieval But we miss another retrieval philosophy metadata And with LLMs its never...

item.title: Approaches to tenancy in Postgres
item.description: There are many ways to slice a Postgres database for multi-tenant applications. Let's look at the three most common approaches and the trade-offs.

item.title: Language Models are Injectiveand Hence Invertible
item.description: Giorgos Nikolaou <sup>1,5,</sup> Tommaso Mencattini <sup>1,2,∗</sup> Donato Crisostomi <sup>2</sup> Andrea Santilli <sup>2</sup> Yannis Panagakis <sup>4,5</sup> Emanuele Rodolà <sup>2,3</sup>

item.title: Harness engineering: leveraging Codex in an agent-first world
item.description: OpenAI built and shipped an internal product with 0 lines of manually-written code. Every line — app logic, tests, CI, docs, observability — written by Codex. Estimated 1/10th the time of hand-written code. Humans steer, agents execute.

item.title: Why Your Agents Can't Read Enterprise Documents — and How to Fix It
item.description: Databricks on why frontier AI agents break down on real enterprise documents: scanned PDFs, messy layouts, mixed formats. IDP (Intelligent Document Processing) is the critical foundation for agentic workflows. Agents handle reasoning fine but can't read the documents they need to reason about.

item.title: The Pulse: ‘Tokenmaxxing’ as a weird new trend
item.description: … which will probably be the shortest-lived trend because it’s so wasteful. Also: coding AI agent subsidies could be ending, Cal.com going closed source and blaming it on AI, and more.

item.title: Using Claude Code: session management and 1M context
item.description: Anthropic on managing Claude Code sessions with the new 1M context window. Covers session strategies, when to compact vs start fresh, subagent usage, and the new /usage command. Lots of variance in how users manage sessions.

item.title: Mechanical sympathy
item.description: On the connection between good engineering and good product design. Truly good engineers understand the contract between creator and user. The concept of affordances, mechanical sympathy, and why understanding how your system is used matters as much as how it works.

item.title: The impact of AI on software engineers in 2026: key trends
item.description: Our AI tooling survey finds concerns about mounting AI costs, more engineers hitting usage limits, and AI tools having uneven effects upon different types of engineers

item.title: Inside Meta's Home Grown AI Analytics Agent
item.description: Meta built an internal AI analytics agent that autonomously runs SQL queries, diagnoses metric drops, and traces root causes. Started as a hack by one data scientist, grew into a company-wide tool. The agent uses query history for context and can handle routine data analysis tasks that data scientists get asked repeatedly.

item.title: Late interaction models: How to scale & optimize in Elasticsearch
item.description: Explore techniques to scale late interaction models like ColPali for large-scale vector search in Elasticsearch.

item.title: ColPali & Elasticsearch: How to search complex documents
item.description: Learn about ColPali and explore how to use it to search through complex documents in Elasticsearch, including tables, figures, multiple columns & more.

item.title: Unsupervised document clustering with Elasticsearch + Jina embeddings
item.description: A practical, reproducible approach to unsupervised document clustering with Elasticsearch and Jina embeddings.

item.title: Beyond RAG for Agent Memory: Retrieval by Decoupling and Aggregation
item.description: Zhanghao Hu Qinglin Zhu Hanqi Yan Yulan He Lin Gui

item.title: Benchmarking AI Agent Memory: Is a Filesystem All You Need?
item.description: Letta Filesystem scores 74.0% of the LoCoMo benchmark by simply storing conversational histories in a file, beating out specialized memory tool libraries.

item.title: ByteRover: Agent-Native Memory Through LLM-Curated Hierarchical Context
item.description: Andy Nguyen <sup>1</sup>, Danh Doan <sup>1</sup>, Hoang Pham <sup>1</sup>, Bao Ha <sup>1</sup>, Dat Pham <sup>1</sup>, Linh Nguyen <sup>1</sup>,

item.title: The Price of Meaning: Why RAG, Knowledge Graphs, and Every Semantic Memory Will Always Fail
item.description: TL;DRForgetting and false recall are mathematically, provably, inevitable for any memory system that organises information by meaning. Not j...

item.title: An Overview of Late Interaction Retrieval Models: ColBERT, ColPali, and ColQwen
item.description: Late interaction allow for semantically rich interactions that enable a precise retrieval process across different modalities of unstructured data, including text and images.

item.title: Geometry of Forgetting: Why Brains and LLMs Fail EXACTLY the Same Way
item.description: TL;DRMemory systems for LLMs forget EXACTLY like humans, reproducing the exact numbers from some of the most replicated experiments in clini...

item.title: Giving AI Agents the Document Understanding Layer They've Been Missing
item.description: Most agent runtimes reduce complex documents to garbled text, degrading every downstream task. LlamaParse and LiteParse are installable agent skills that deliver structured, layout-aware, multimodal document parsing — locally or via cloud — so agents can finally reason over real enterprise documents.

item.title: Introducing Context Repositories: Git-based Memory for Coding Agents
item.description: We're introducing Context Repositories, a rebuild of how memory works in Letta Code based on programmatic context management and git-based versioning.

item.title: Engineering Insights: Failure Modes That Break VLM-Powered OCR in Production
item.description: Two hidden LLM failure modes that break agentic document ingestion pipelines in production: repetition loops exhaust resources while recitation filters block legitimate tasks. Learn how to detect, handle, and mitigate both across OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini APIs.

item.title: DHH’s new way of writing code
item.description: David Heinemeier Hansson shares why he shifted to an agent-first AI workflow, and what it means for how software is built and who builds it.

item.title: Cycles of disruption in the tech industry: with software pioneers Kent Beck & Martin Fowler
item.description: Parallels between technology shifts in the past decades and what we’re seeing with AI. Also: ways to avoid burnout when working with AI agents, TDD back in style, and more.

item.title: Database retrieval tools for context engineering
item.description: Best practices for writing database retrieval tools for context engineering. Learn how to design and evaluate agent tools for interacting with Elasticsearch data.

item.title: Commits · milla-jovovich/mempalace
item.description: The highest-scoring AI memory system ever benchmarked. And it's free. - Commits · milla-jovovich/mempalace

item.title: How we built a virtual filesystem for our Assistant
item.description: We replaced expensive sandboxes with ChromaFs, a virtual filesystem over Chroma, to give our docs AI assistant the ability to explore documentation like a developer would.

item.title: Harnessing Claude's Intelligence | 3 Key Patterns for Building Apps
item.description: Three patterns for building on the Claude Platform that keep pace with Claude's evolving intelligence while balancing latency and cost.

item.title: Components of A Coding Agent
item.description: How coding agents use tools, memory, and repo context to make LLMs work better in practice

item.title: FUSE — The Linux Kernel documentation
item.description: Userspace filesystem:

item.title: Lessons from Building Claude Code: Seeing like an Agent
item.description: One of the hardest parts of building an agent harness is constructing its action space.Claude acts through Tool Calling, but there are a num...

item.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
item.description: Murphy Campbell has an AI imposter on Spotify and troll claiming copyright of her performances of public domain ballads on YouTube.

item.title: The Pulse: Industry leaders return to coding with AI
item.description: Mark Zuckerberg and Garry Tan join the trend of C-level folks jumping back into coding with AI. Also: a bad week for Claude Code and GitHub, and more

item.title: Scaling Uber with Thuan Pham (Uber’s first CTO)
item.description: Thuan Pham (Uber's first CTO) on scaling Uber from constant outages to global infrastructure, the shift to microservices and platform teams, and how AI is reshaping engineering.

item.title: What is inference engineering? Deepdive
item.description: Many engineers use inference daily, but inference engineering is a bit obscure – and an area rich with interesting challenges. Philip Kiely, author of the new book, “Inference Engineering,” explains

item.title: The Pulse: is GitHub still best for AI-native development?
item.description: Poor availability has dogged GitHub for months and raises questions about its status and focus. Plus, Microsoft promises Windows will not be “Microslop”, a massive LLM supply chain attack, and more

item.title: “How to be a 10x engineer” – interview with a standout dev
item.description: An interview with an engineer with no public GitHub contributions, setting clear boundaries – and yet not having needed to apply for positions when searching for a job, because referrals found them

item.title: The Pulse: Is the FDE role becoming less desirable?
item.description: Also: AI-agent generated pull requests cause headaches for large open source projects, OpenAI acquires the creator of uv, a sudden Cursor price hike annoys some enterprise customers, and more

item.title: Building WhatsApp with Jean Lee
item.description: Jean Lee, engineer #19 at WhatsApp, on scaling the app with a tiny team, the Facebook acquisition, and what it reveals about the future of engineering.

item.title: Are AI agents actually slowing us down?
item.description: As more software engineers use AI agents daily, there’s also more sloppy software, outages, quality issues, and even a slowdown in shipping velocity. What’s happening, and how do we solve it?

item.title: The Pulse: What will the Staff Engineer role look like in 2027 and beyond?
item.description: Also: new trend of token costs becoming a worry for CTOs, 10% cuts at Atlassian, and more.

item.title: From IDEs to AI Agents with Steve Yegge
item.description: Steve Yegge on how AI is reshaping software engineering, the rise of “vibe coding,” and why developers must adapt to a rapidly changing craft.

item.title: How Uber uses AI for development: inside look
item.description: How Uber built Minion, Shepherd, uReview, and other internal agentic AI tools. Also, new challenges in rolling out AI tools, like more platform investment and growing concern about token costs

item.title: The Pulse: AWS region knocked offline by drone attack in historic first
item.description: Also: 40% job cuts at Block “not due to AI”, Antigravity bans reinforce Google’s disdain for paying customers, and more

item.title: Building Claude Code with Boris Cherny
item.description: Claude Code creator Boris Cherny on building AI-powered coding tools, parallel agents, and how the engineer's role is evolving in an AI-first world.

item.title: AI Tooling for Software Engineers in 2026
item.description: Claude Code dominates tool usage, leaders are more positive about AI than engineers, staff+ engineers are the biggest users of AI agents, and more. Exclusive data and analysis from 900+ respondents

item.title: The Pulse: Cloudflare rewrites Next.js as AI rewrites commercial open source
item.description: An engineer at Cloudflare rewrote most of Vercel’s Next.js in one week with AI agents. It looks like a sign of how AI will disrupt existing moats and business models. Analysis

item.title: Mitchell Hashimoto’s new way of writing code
item.description: Mitchell Hashimoto on building HashiCorp, navigating the cloud giants, and how AI agents have transformed his day-to-day engineering workflow.

item.title: The Future of Software Engineering with AI: Six Predictions
item.description: Notes from The Pragmatic Summit and ‘The Future of Software Development’ workshop

item.title: The Pulse #162: Even fewer middle managers and more flexible teams?
item.description: Also: Anthropic bans third-party devs while Codex embraces them, Peter Steinberger joins OpenAI, a lot more software gets created with AI, and more
